senses.definition: Coming from outside rather than being a natural or basic part of something.
examples: The disease was thought to be caused by advenient factors in the environment.
These advenient influences changed the community’s traditional way of life.

senses.definition: Coming to or arriving at something (present participle of an obsolete verb, used only in very formal or historical contexts).
examples: Writers once spoke of blessings advening from above.
The advening of such changes was feared by traditionalists.

senses.definition: The arrival or beginning of an important event, person, idea, or time.
examples: The advent of the internet changed how people communicate.
With the advent of spring, the fields turned green.

senses.definition: In the Christian calendar, the four weeks before Christmas that are used as a time of waiting and preparation.
examples: Many churches light a candle each week of Advent.
During Advent, families prepared for the celebration of Christmas.

senses.definition: Happening by chance or added from outside rather than being an essential part of something (used rarely, like "adventitious").
examples: The advential influences on the experiment were carefully controlled.
Such advential factors are not central to the main argument.

senses.definition: A set of Christian beliefs that strongly focus on the second coming of Christ, especially those of the Seventh-day Adventist Church and related movements.
examples: She studied the history of Adventism at university.
Adventism spread rapidly in the nineteenth century.

senses.definition: A member or supporter of a Christian group that believes strongly in the second coming of Christ, especially the Seventh-day Adventist Church.
examples: Her grandparents were devoted Adventists.
Adventists often place special importance on health and diet.

senses.definition: Relating to Adventists or their beliefs.
examples: He grew up in an Adventist family.
The hospital is run by an Adventist organization.

senses.definition: Plural of Adventist: members or supporters of Christian groups that expect the second coming of Christ, especially the Seventh-day Adventist Church.
examples: Adventists have founded many schools and hospitals worldwide.
In that region, Adventists are a small minority.

senses.definition: The outer layer of connective tissue that surrounds a blood vessel or another organ in the body.
examples: The artery’s adventitia contains nerves and small blood vessels.
Damage to the adventitia can weaken the vessel wall.

senses.definition: Relating to the adventitia, the outer tissue layer of a blood vessel or organ.
examples: Inflammation was observed in the adventitial layer of the artery.
The adventitial tissue contains small nerves and vessels.

senses.definition: Plural of adventitia: outer connective tissue layers around blood vessels or organs.
examples: The surgeon examined the adventitias of the affected veins.
Different arteries have adventitias of varying thickness.

senses.definition: Happening by chance or in a way that is not planned or essential.
examples: Their meeting was completely adventitious but led to a lifelong friendship.
The success was partly due to adventitious circumstances.

senses.definition: Growing or appearing in an unusual or not normal position, especially in plants or animals.
examples: The plant develops adventitious roots from its stems.
Adventitious buds can form on damaged branches.

senses.definition: In a way that happens by chance or is not a basic or planned part of something.
examples: He became involved in the project adventitiously, through a chance phone call.
The company grew adventitiously as new markets unexpectedly opened.

senses.definition: The quality of happening by chance or being added from outside rather than being essential.
examples: The adventitiousness of their success became clear in later years.
He stressed that any adventitiousness in the results must be carefully examined.

senses.definition: Not native to a place and not yet fully established there, especially about plants or animals.
examples: Many adventive plants appear along railway lines.
The species is considered adventive rather than fully naturalized.

senses.definition: A non-native plant or animal that appears in a new area but is not yet fully established there.
examples: These adventives may die out if conditions become less favorable.
The botanist recorded several adventives along the coast.

senses.definition: In a way that appears from outside or in a place where it is not native or usual.
examples: The plant spread adventively along the disturbed roadside.
These organisms arrived adventively, carried by ships and vehicles.

senses.definition: Plural of adventive: non-native plants or animals that appear in a new area but are not fully established.
examples: The checklist notes several adventives in the region.
These adventives may become invasive if conditions change.

senses.definition: An adventure or enterprise, especially a bold undertaking (now rare or historical).
examples: The knight set out on a dangerous adventry.
Such an adventry would test the courage of any sailor.

senses.definition: Plural of advent: important arrivals or beginnings of events, times, or developments.
examples: The advents of radio and television transformed communication.
Several technological advents reshaped the industry.

senses.definition: Relating to the Christian season of Advent.
examples: The church held several adventual services before Christmas.
Adventual traditions vary between different denominations.

senses.definition: An unusual, exciting, and sometimes risky experience or activity.
examples: They went on an adventure through the jungle.
Moving to another country was a big adventure for her.

senses.definition: To risk something or to do something that involves danger or uncertainty, often in search of something new.
examples: They decided to adventure into the mountains without a guide.
Few companies would adventure so much capital on a single project.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of adventure: risked something or did something involving danger or uncertainty.
examples: They adventured all their savings on the new business.
He adventured into unknown territory in search of gold.

senses.definition: Full of adventure or exciting experiences.
examples: They enjoyed an adventureful summer in the mountains.
His life had been long and adventureful.

senses.definition: The act of venturing or taking a risk; an adventure (now rare or historical).
examples: By this adventurement he gained both wealth and fame.
Such adventurement was uncommon among merchants of his time.

senses.definition: Someone who enjoys taking risks and having new, exciting experiences, often by traveling.
examples: She is an adventurer who has climbed mountains on every continent.
The book tells the story of a young adventurer at sea.

senses.definition: Someone who takes big risks to gain money or power, sometimes in a dishonest way.
examples: The company fell into the hands of financial adventurers.
He was seen as an adventurer rather than a responsible leader.

senses.definition: Plural of adventurer: people who enjoy risk and new, exciting experiences, often through travel or exploration.
examples: The island attracted adventurers from around the world.
Many adventurers joined the expedition into the rainforest.

senses.definition: Plural of adventure: unusual, exciting, and sometimes risky experiences or activities.
examples: His travels were full of adventures and surprises.
They shared stories of their adventures abroad.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being adventurous or engaged in adventure (very rare).
examples: A sense of adventureship drove them to explore unknown lands.
His letters reveal a spirit of pure adventureship.

senses.definition: Willing to take risks or try new and exciting things.
examples: She has an adventuresome personality and loves extreme sports.
The menu includes some adventuresome dishes for curious diners.

senses.definition: In a way that shows a willingness to take risks or try new, exciting things.
examples: They adventuresomely chose the most difficult route up the mountain.
He adventuresomely invested in a small, unknown company.

senses.definition: The quality of liking risk and new, exciting experiences.
examples: His adventuresomeness led him to travel the world alone.
The film celebrates the adventuresomeness of youth.

senses.definition: Plural of adventuresomeness: different forms or examples of being willing to take risks and seek new experiences.
examples: The characters in the novel display various adventuresomenesses.
Different cultures express their adventuresomenesses in distinct ways.

senses.definition: Informal plural or playful use referring to people who are adventuresome.
examples: The adventuresomes in the group chose the harder trail.
Only the true adventuresomes signed up for the winter expedition.

senses.definition: A woman who enjoys adventure and taking risks, especially by traveling or trying new things.
examples: As an adventuress, she explored remote islands alone.
The film follows a fearless adventuress on her journey.

senses.definition: A woman who tries to gain money or social position by using charm or risky, sometimes dishonest methods.
examples: In the novel, a clever adventuress marries into a rich family.
He warned his friend that she might be an adventuress after his fortune.

senses.definition: Plural of adventuress: women who seek adventure or, in some contexts, who try to gain money or status through risky or manipulative behavior.
examples: The story tells of adventuresses who travel the world together.
He had a reputation for falling in love with adventuresses.

senses.definition: Taking risks or going to new and possibly dangerous places in search of excitement or gain.
examples: They spent their twenties adventuring around the world.
Adventuring in unknown waters was part of his job as a sailor.

senses.definition: The activity of seeking adventure and taking risks.
examples: Adventuring is easier today thanks to modern transport.
He wrote a blog about his life of adventuring.

senses.definition: Somewhat adventurous or giving the feeling of adventure.
examples: The trail looked a bit adventurish for beginner hikers.
He gave her an adventurish smile before suggesting a road trip.

senses.definition: Risky or reckless behavior, especially in politics or international relations, that ignores accepted rules or careful planning.
examples: The opposition accused the government of dangerous adventurism.
Military adventurism abroad can lead to long conflicts.

senses.definition: Plural of adventurism: different kinds or examples of reckless, risk-taking behavior, especially in politics or finance.
examples: The book analyzes the various adventurisms of the regime.
Their financial adventurisms nearly ruined the company.

senses.definition: Someone, especially a politician or leader, who takes big, risky actions without enough care or planning.
examples: Critics called him an adventurist for starting the conflict.
An economic adventurist can put millions of jobs at risk.

senses.definition: Describing people or actions that are recklessly risk-taking, especially in politics or international affairs.
examples: They warned against adventurist policies in the region.
Such an adventurist move could damage relations with neighbors.

senses.definition: Showing or based on adventurism; willing to take large, often careless risks, especially in politics or business.
examples: Their adventuristic foreign policy worried many allies.
The CEO’s adventuristic decisions led to heavy losses.

senses.definition: Plural of adventurist: people, especially leaders, who take big, risky actions without enough care or planning.
examples: The adventurists within the party pushed for radical change.
Financial adventurists often ignore the long-term consequences.

senses.definition: Willing to take risks or try new and exciting things.
examples: She is very adventurous and loves backpacking alone.
If you feel adventurous, you can try the spicy dish.

senses.definition: Involving new, exciting, or possibly risky experiences or ideas.
examples: They planned an adventurous route across the desert.
The restaurant offers an adventurous menu.

senses.definition: In a way that shows a love of risk and new, exciting experiences.
examples: She lived adventurously, moving from country to country.
They adventurously chose the longest and hardest trail.

senses.definition: The quality of liking risk and new, exciting experiences.
examples: His adventurousness inspired his friends to travel more.
The brand’s image is built on youth and adventurousness.

senses.definition: Plural of adventurousness: different forms or examples of a tendency to take risks and try new experiences.
examples: Their various adventurousnesses shaped the company’s culture.
The book compares the adventurousnesses of different explorers.

senses.definition: A word that adds information about how, when, where, or to what degree an action happens, or about an adjective or another adverb.
examples: In the sentence "She ran quickly", the word "quickly" is an adverb.
Words like "very" and "quite" are common adverbs.
collocations: modify a verb with an adverb

senses.definition: Relating to an adverb or behaving like an adverb in a sentence.
examples: In the phrase "in the morning", the words have an adverbial function.
She underlined the adverbial phrase in the exercise.

senses.definition: A word or group of words in a sentence that functions like an adverb.
examples: "In the garden" is an adverbial in the sentence "The children played in the garden."
The teacher asked the class to identify all the adverbials.

senses.definition: To make something function as an adverb or to treat something as adverbial, especially in grammatical description (chiefly British and technical).
examples: The suffix can adverbialise the adjective in some contexts.
This construction adverbialises the whole phrase.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of adverbialise: made to function as an adverb or treated as adverbial in grammar.
examples: In that sentence, the noun is adverbialised by its position.
The phrase can be adverbialised to modify the verb.

senses.definition: Third person singular of adverbialise: makes something function as an adverb or treats it as adverbial in grammar.
examples: This pattern adverbialises the whole clause.
The suffix often adverbialises the adjective in English.
